title: Fountain Channel
desc: A public fountain in the outer ring is running weak, which means somebody needs to follow the water before the queue grows sour.

steps.text:
Public fountains in Kiru City's outer ring do not merely provide water. They provide reassurance. A working basin means the channels are sound, the cistern clerks are awake, and the district has not slipped out of somebody's map. That is why the line forms quickly when one fountain starts coughing instead of pouring. The basin still runs, but only in uncertain spurts that make every waiting bucket feel like a mild accusation.

The channel keeper lifts the stone grate with a hooked rod and peers into the dark runoff slot. "Clear the inlet grates if you want to follow the blockage directly," she says. "Or reseat the spillway tiles around the basin edge if you would rather fix what the weak pressure has already loosened. One finds the problem. The other keeps the fountain from looking like it has one."

options.label: Lift the inlet grates and clear the channel throat
Reseat the loose spillway tiles around the basin

steps.text:
The inlet throat is half-full of leaf pulp, roof grit, and the kind of ordinary city debris that becomes extraordinary the moment water tries to pass through it. You work the rod through the narrow stone run, draw out the worst of the clog by hand, and flush the remainder with two test buckets until the sound changes from reluctant dripping to a proper enclosed rush. A second later the basin answers with a stronger sheet of water that makes the waiting line visibly relax.

The keeper gives the stream a professional look, which is only slightly less demanding than an offended one. This time it satisfies her. The fountain spills steady, the queue resumes in something like order, and the district's mood improves by several quiet degrees. "There," she says. "Nothing turns public confidence around faster than a pipe remembering its duty."

options.label: Lower the grate and leave the water running cleanly

steps.text:
Weak pressure has let the spillway edge dry unevenly, and two tiles have shifted just enough to make the whole basin look neglected. You lift each loose piece, scrape the old grit from the setting bed, and tap it back into line so the lip reads as one continuous curve instead of a sequence of excuses. Once the basin edge is true again, the thin water sheet stops breaking into ragged little falls and begins sliding cleanly into the channel trough below.

It is a small change until you step back. Then the fountain looks right again, which matters more than most people admit. Buckets fill beneath a calm, even spill. The keeper runs her thumb along the reset seam and nods. "Good. If a city wants to feel stable, its stone has to agree with its water."

options.label: Brush the last grit from the rim and reopen the basin
